text: For low hemoglobin, include iron-rich foods like lentils, spinach, beans, and lean red meat with vitamin C sources to improve absorption.
For high glucose, reduce refined sugars, prioritize whole grains and vegetables, and target at least 150 minutes of moderate weekly activity.
Abnormal TSH may require clinician follow-up; maintain regular sleep, avoid abrupt iodine supplement changes, and monitor symptoms consistently.
For elevated LDL or total cholesterol, increase soluble fiber intake, reduce trans fats, and emphasize unsaturated fats from nuts and fish.
For high triglycerides, limit alcohol and added sugars, choose complex carbs, and include omega-3 rich foods such as salmon and walnuts.
For low vitamin D, combine safe sunlight exposure with dietary sources like fortified milk, eggs, and fatty fish, and discuss supplementation with a clinician.
For low vitamin B12, include dairy, eggs, fish, and fortified cereals; vegetarians may need targeted supplementation under medical guidance.
Any persistent abnormal biomarker should be re-tested and interpreted with a healthcare professional in the context of symptoms and medical history.
